From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Method of dismissal in cricket

This article is about the method of dismissal in cricket. For other uses, see [Stumped (disambiguation)](/wiki/Stumped_(disambiguation) "Stumped (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Hayden_and_Dhoni.jpg/250px-Hayden_and_Dhoni.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hayden_and_Dhoni.jpg)

Indian wicketkeeper [M. S. Dhoni](/wiki/M._S._Dhoni "M. S. Dhoni") appeals for a stumping against Australian batsman [Matthew Hayden](/wiki/Matthew_Hayden "Matthew Hayden")

**Stumped** is a method of [dismissing](/wiki/Dismissal_(cricket) "Dismissal (cricket)") a [batter](/wiki/Batter_(cricket) "Batter (cricket)") in [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket"), in which the [wicket-keeper](/wiki/Wicket-keeper "Wicket-keeper") [puts down the wicket](/wiki/Put_down_the_wicket "Put down the wicket") of the [striker](/wiki/Glossary_of_cricket_terms#S "Glossary of cricket terms") while the striker is out of their [ground](/wiki/Batter%27s_ground "Batter's ground").[[1]](#cite_note-Law39-1) It is governed by Law 39 of the [Laws of Cricket](/wiki/Laws_of_Cricket "Laws of Cricket").[[1]](#cite_note-Law39-1)

Being "out of their ground" means no part of the batter's body, equipment or bat is touching the ground behind the [crease](/wiki/Popping_crease "Popping crease").

Stumped is a special case of [run out](/wiki/Run_out_(cricket) "Run out (cricket)"), but a stumping can only be affected by the [wicket-keeper](/wiki/Wicket-keeper "Wicket-keeper") without the intervention of another fielder, when the striker is not attempting a [run](/wiki/Run_(cricket) "Run (cricket)"), and the ball must not be a [no-ball](/wiki/No-ball "No-ball"). If the criteria for both stumped and run out are met, then the dismissal will be recorded as a stumping and credited to the bowler and wicket-keeper.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

As always in cricket, one of the fielding team must [appeal](/wiki/Appeal_(cricket) "Appeal (cricket)") for the wicket by asking the [umpires](/wiki/Umpire_(cricket) "Umpire (cricket)"). It is the striker's end umpire who adjudicates all stumpings, and all run-out appeals at the striker's end.

## Practice

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stumped&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Practice")]

Stumping is the fifth-most-common form of dismissal after [caught](/wiki/Caught "Caught"), [bowled](/wiki/Bowled "Bowled"), [leg before wicket](/wiki/Leg_before_wicket "Leg before wicket"), and [run out](/wiki/Run_out "Run out"),[[3]](#cite_note-fifth-3) though it is seen more commonly in shorter forms of the game such as [Twenty20](/wiki/Twenty20 "Twenty20") cricket, because attacking batting leads both to batters more frequently losing their balance, and to deliberately leaving the crease to better strike the ball.

Most stumping dismissals are affected by the keeper ["standing up"](/wiki/Glossary_of_cricket_terms#S "Glossary of cricket terms") (i.e. able to [put down the wicket](/wiki/Wicket#Putting_down_a_wicket "Wicket") by reaching out with the gloved hand holding the ball) and so are usually taken by a medium or slow [bowler](/wiki/Bowler_(cricket) "Bowler (cricket)"), especially a slower [spin bowler](/wiki/Spin_bowling "Spin bowling"). If the keeper is "standing back" to a faster bowler, the wicket-keeper may throw the ball at the stumps to remove the [bails](/wiki/Bail_(cricket) "Bail (cricket)") ("throw down the wicket") and is also credited with the stumping, although any other fielder throwing down the wicket would be a [run out](/wiki/Run_out "Run out").

Stumping is often an intended consequence of the fielding team's setup and involves cooperation between bowler and wicket-keeper: the bowler draws the batter out of their ground, such as by delivering a ball with a shorter length to tempt the batter to step forward to create a [half-volley](/wiki/Glossary_of_cricket_terms#H "Glossary of cricket terms"), and when they miss the ball, the keeper catches it and breaks the wicket before the batter "makes their ground" (i.e. places the bat or part of their body on the ground back behind the [popping crease](/wiki/Crease_(cricket) "Crease (cricket)")).

If the bails are removed before the act of stumping (e.g by the keeper's foot), the batter can still be stumped if the wicket-keeper removes one of the stumps from the ground, while holding the ball in the hand or arm that is in contact with the stump.

A batter may be out stumped off a [wide](/wiki/Wide_(cricket) "Wide (cricket)") delivery,[[4]](#cite_note-4) in which case the batting team are still credited with the one run extra and both umpires are required to signal to the scorers: the bowler's end umpire signals the Wide, the striker's end, that the batsman is out.

A batter cannot be stumped off a [no-ball](/wiki/No_ball "No ball") (as stumped is credited to the bowler, the fielding team cannot benefit from the illegal delivery).

Notes:

* The popping crease is defined as the back edge of the crease marking (i.e. the edge closer to the wicket. Therefore, a batter whose bat or foot is *on* the crease marking, but does not touch the ground *behind* the crease marking, can be stumped. This is quite common if the batter's back foot is raised so that only their toe is on the ground.
* The wicket must be properly put down in accordance with Law 29 of the [Laws of cricket](/wiki/Laws_of_cricket "Laws of cricket"): using either the ball itself or a hand or arm that is in contact with the ball at the point of breaking the wicket. A stumping is also valid if the ball merely rebounds from the wicket-keeper and breaks the wicket just as if the wicket had been thrown down.
* The wicket-keeper must allow the ball to pass the stumps before taking it, unless it has touched either the batter or his bat first (Law 21.9).[[5]](#cite_note-5) If the wicket-keeper fails to do this, the delivery is a [no-ball](/wiki/No_ball "No ball"), and the batter cannot be stumped (nor run out, unless he attempts to run to the other wicket[[6]](#cite_note-6)).
* If the striker is injured and has a runner (no longer permitted in international cricket or many professional competitions, but within the unmodified Laws of Cricket), the runner leaves their crease and the wicket is put down for what would otherwise be a stumping, the striker is run out, not stumped.
